prompt:
Write a Python function edit(commands) that simulates a one-line text editor and returns the final state as a tuple (text, cursor).

The buffer starts empty and the cursor starts at position 0 (the cursor sits between characters; position k means k characters are to its left).

commands is a list of strings, processed left to right:
- "type <text>": insert <text> at the cursor; the cursor ends immediately after the inserted text. <text> is everything after the first space and may itself contain further spaces; it must be non-empty.
- "left <n>" / "right <n>": move the cursor n positions, clamping at 0 and at the end of the buffer. Clamping is not an error.
- "backspace <n>": delete up to n characters immediately before the cursor (fewer if the cursor has fewer than n characters to its left); the cursor moves left by the number of characters actually deleted.
- "undo": revert the most recent not-yet-undone command that actually changed the buffer, restoring BOTH the buffer AND the cursor to their values from immediately before that command (cursor motion performed after that command is discarded). A backspace that deleted zero characters did not change the buffer and is never an undo target. "undo" when nothing is undoable is a no-op. Undo itself can never be undone.

Raise ValueError for a malformed command at the moment it is processed:
- unknown verb, or a verb with no argument (e.g. "left" alone, "type" alone);
- "type" with an empty payload ("type " with nothing after the space);
- left/right/backspace whose argument is not a non-empty string of ASCII digits ("-2", "+3", "2x", "2 3", and "" are all malformed) or whose numeric value is 0;
- "undo" followed by anything (e.g. "undo 2").

The input list must not be mutated. Return the result as a tuple (text, cursor).

Output ONLY the function definition.
